Review the changes on branch `DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3` of the repo at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator` against `origin/master`. The branch has just been rebased/merged with master (merge commit `e2ff48e`). A master merge just landed, so the diff now includes both the original DATA-4715 work and the merge resolution.

Focus exclusively on the DATA-4715 changes (audit log archival to S3), not the master-side Phase-2/Phase-3 work. The key files to review are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py` — S3AuditLogWriter, AuditArchiver, DynamoDBAuditLogger.delete_logs, _batch_write_with_retry, _backoff_seconds
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py` — _archive_if_done, process() success path, merged __init__ signature
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py` — archiver construction and wiring
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py` — DuplicateRequestError catch
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py` — DuplicateRequestError, DynamoDB create() translation
- `template.yaml` — ProcessS3WritePolicy, AuditLogS3Bucket/Prefix params
- `tests/test_audit_logging.py`, `tests/test_s3_audit_log_writer.py`, `tests/test_request_processor.py`, `tests/test_request_repository.py`, `tests/test_creator_lambda.py`, `tests/test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py`

Get the diff with: `git diff origin/master...HEAD -- src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/audit_logging.py src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py template.yaml tests/test_audit_logging.py tests/test_s3_audit_log_writer.py tests/test_request_processor.py tests/test_request_repository.py tests/test_creator_lambda.py tests/test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py`

Also read the full current content of `audit_logging.py` and `request_processor.py` for full context.

Context on what this branch does:
- Adds `S3AuditLogWriter` to archive audit logs as gzipped JSONL when a request reaches `done`
- Adds `AuditArchiver` that orchestrates S3-write-then-DynamoDB-delete
- Adds `DynamoDBAuditLogger.delete_logs()` with chunked `BatchWriteItem` and capped exponential backoff retry (extracted into `_batch_write_with_retry` + `_backoff_seconds`)
- `process()` in `RequestProcessor` calls `_archive_if_done(request)` on the success path after `process_request()`
- `DuplicateRequestError` raised by both repo implementations, caught in `creator_lambda` instead of sniffing `ClientError` codes
- `template.yaml` wires the S3 bucket/prefix params and adds `ProcessS3WritePolicy`
- Serverless-access-manager is used — check that the template follows the house conventions (see `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` usage in the file)

Run the full test suite (`uv run pytest -q`) and type checker (`uv run basedpyright`) to confirm the current state before reviewing.

Provide a thorough review at **high** effort covering: correctness, performance, security, test quality, type precision, README sync, SAM template conventions (including serverless-access-manager patterns), and the new §3m inline-logic clarity dimension. Be specific and include line numbers.

57	class AuditLogger(ABC):
58	    """Base interface for audit logging"""
59	
60	    @abstractmethod
61	    def log(self, id: str, message: str, timestamp: datetime | None = None) -> None:
62	        """
63	        Log an audit log message.
64	
65	        The id should be the same as an erasure requests id
66	
67	        The message can be any free text. You should avoid storing raw personal
68	        information in messages, and make use of anonymisation functions.
69

71	        UTC timezone. All datetimes given will be coereced to UTC.
72	        """

125	class DynamoDBAuditLogger(AuditLogger):
126	    """DynamoDB backed audit logging"""
127	
128	    def __init__(self, table_name: str, dynamodb: DynamoDBClient) -> None:
129	        super().__init__()
130	        self.table_name: str = table_name
131	        self.dynamodb: DynamoDBClient = dynamodb
132	
133	    @override
134	    def log(self, id: str, message: str, timestamp: datetime | None = None) -> None:
135	        if timestamp is None:
136	            timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
137	        else:
138	            timestamp = timestamp.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
139	        item = self._create_item(id, message, timestamp)
140	        _ = self.dynamodb.put_item(
141	            TableName=self.table_name,
142	            Item=item,
143	            ReturnValues="NONE",
144	        )
145	        logger.info("Audit Message sent: %s", message)
146	
147	    @override
148	    def get_logs(self, id: str) -> list[AuditEntry]:
149	        paginator = self.dynamodb.get_paginator("query")
150	        pages = paginator.paginate(
151	            TableName=self.table_name,
152	            KeyConditionExpression="#id = :id",
153	            ExpressionAttributeNames={"#id": AuditEntry.ID_FIELD},
154	            ExpressionAttributeValues={":id": {"S": id}},
155	            ScanIndexForward=True,
156	        )
157	        results: list[AuditEntry] = []
158	        for page in pages:
159	            if "Items" not in page:
160	                continue
161	            for item in page["Items"]:
162	                results.append(self._item_to_entry(item))
163	        return results
164	
165	    def delete_logs(self, id: str) -> None:
166	        """Delete all audit log entries for the given erasure id from DynamoDB."""
167	        paginator = self.dynamodb.get_paginator("query")
168	        pages = paginator.paginate(
169	            TableName=self.table_name,
170	            KeyConditionExpression="#id = :id",
171	            ExpressionAttributeNames={
172	                "#id": AuditEntry.ID_FIELD,
173	                "#ts": AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD,
174	            },
175	            ExpressionAttributeValues={":id": {"S": id}},
176	            ProjectionExpression="#id, #ts",
177	        )
178	        keys: list[dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef]] = []
179	        for page in pages:
180	            for item in page.get("Items", []):
181	                keys.append(
182	                    {
183	                        AuditEntry.ID_FIELD: item[AuditEntry.ID_FIELD],
184	                        AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD: item[AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD],
185	                    }
186	                )
187	        for i in range(0, len(keys), DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS):
188	            chunk = keys[i : i + DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS]
189	            request_items: dict[str, list[WriteRequestTypeDef]] = {
190	                self.table_name: [{"DeleteRequest": {"Key": key}} for key in chunk]
191	            }
192	            self._batch_write_with_retry(request_items)
193	
194	    def _batch_write_with_retry(
195	        self, request_items: "dict[str, list[WriteRequestTypeDef]]"
196	    ) -> None:
197	        """Issue a BatchWriteItem request, retrying unprocessed items with backoff."""
198	        for attempt in range(DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS):
199	            response = self.dynamodb.batch_write_item(RequestItems=request_items)
200	            unprocessed = response.get("UnprocessedItems") or {}
201	            if not unprocessed:
202	                return
203	            # Cast: AWS returns UnprocessedItems in the same shape accepted by RequestItems
204	            request_items = cast("dict[str, list[WriteRequestTypeDef]]", unprocessed)
205	            time.sleep(_backoff_seconds(attempt))
206	        raise RuntimeError(
207	            f"BatchWriteItem still has unprocessed deletes for {self.table_name} after {DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS} attempts"
208	        )

246	class S3AuditLogReader:
247	    """Reads materialised audit logs from S3.
248	
249	    Each erasure request's complete audit log is stored as a single gzipped
250	    JSON Lines object at ``s3://{bucket}/{prefix}/{id}.jsonl.gz``.
251	    """
252	
253	    def __init__(self, bucket: str, prefix: str, s3_client: S3Client) -> None:
254	        self.bucket: str = bucket
255	        self.prefix: str = prefix.strip("/")
256	        self.s3: S3Client = s3_client
257	
258	    def _key_for(self, id: str) -> str:
259	        return _s3_key_for(self.prefix, id)
260	
261	    def get_logs(self, id: str) -> list[AuditEntry]:
262	        key = self._key_for(id)
263	        try:
264	            response = self.s3.get_object(Bucket=self.bucket, Key=key)
265	        except ClientError as e:
266	            error_code = e.response.get("Error", {}).get("Code", "")
267	            if error_code in ("NoSuchKey", "404", "NoSuchBucket"):
268	                return []
269	            raise
270	
271	        body_bytes: bytes = response["Body"].read()
272	        decompressed = gzip.decompress(body_bytes).decode("utf-8")
273	        entries: list[AuditEntry] = []
274	        for line in decompressed.splitlines():
275	            stripped = line.strip()
276	            if not stripped:
277	                continue
278	            entries.append(
279	                AuditEntry.from_json(json.loads(stripped))  # pyright: ignore[reportAny]
280	            )
281	        return entries
282	
283	
284	class FallbackAuditLogReader:
285	    """Reads audit logs from a primary source, falling back to S3 when empty."""
286	
287	    def __init__(self, primary: AuditLogger, fallback: S3AuditLogReader) -> None:
288	        self.primary: AuditLogger = primary
289	        self.fallback: S3AuditLogReader = fallback
290	
291	    def get_logs(self, id: str) -> list[AuditEntry]:
292	        primary_results = self.primary.get_logs(id)
293	        if primary_results:
294	            return primary_results
295	        logger.info(
296	            "No audit logs found in primary store for id %s, falling back to S3", id
297	        )
298	        return self.fallback.get_logs(id)
299	
300	
301	class S3AuditLogWriter:
302	    """Writes a materialised audit log for a single erasure id to S3.
303	
304	    Produces the identical gzipped JSON Lines format that ``S3AuditLogReader``
305	    consumes, so ``FallbackAuditLogReader`` continues to work without changes.
306	    Entries are sorted by timestamp (ascending) before being written, so the
307	    on-disk JSONL is always in chronological order regardless of input order.
308	    """
309	
310	    def __init__(self, bucket: str, prefix: str, s3_client: S3Client) -> None:
311	        self.bucket: str = bucket
312	        self.prefix: str = prefix.strip("/")
313	        self.s3: S3Client = s3_client
314	
315	    def write(self, id: str, entries: list[AuditEntry]) -> None:
316	        key = _s3_key_for(self.prefix, id)
317	        sorted_entries = sorted(entries, key=lambda e: e.timestamp)
318	        lines = "\n".join(json.dumps(entry.to_json()) for entry in sorted_entries)
319	        body = gzip.compress(lines.encode("utf-8"))
320	        _ = self.s3.put_object(Bucket=self.bucket, Key=key, Body=body)
321	        logger.info(
322	            "Archived %d audit log entries to s3://%s/%s",
323	            len(sorted_entries),
324	            self.bucket,
325	            key,
326	        )
327	
328	
329	class AuditArchiver:
330	    """Archives audit logs for an erasure request to S3 then removes them from DynamoDB.
331	
332	    Write order is intentional: S3 first, then DynamoDB delete. If the delete
333	    fails mid-way the DynamoDB copy still exists so ``FallbackAuditLogReader``
334	    will never return empty results. Retrying ``archive`` is safe — the S3
335	    object is overwritten and ``delete_logs`` tolerates already-deleted items.
336	    """
337	
338	    def __init__(self, primary: DynamoDBAuditLogger, writer: S3AuditLogWriter) -> None:
339	        self.primary: DynamoDBAuditLogger = primary
340	        self.writer: S3AuditLogWriter = writer
341	
342	    def archive(self, id: str) -> None:
343	        entries = self.primary.get_logs(id)
344	        if not entries:
345	            logger.info("No audit log entries to archive for id %s", id)
346	            return
347	        self.writer.write(id, entries)
348	        self.primary.delete_logs(id)
349	        logger.info("Audit log for id %s archived and removed from DynamoDB", id)
350	
351	
352	DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS: Final[int] = 25  # AWS BatchWriteItem hard limit per call
353	DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS: Final[int] = 5
354	BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS: Final[float] = 0.1
355	BACKOFF_MAX_SECONDS: Final[float] = 2.0
356	
357	
358	def _backoff_seconds(attempt: int) -> float:
359	    """Capped exponential backoff: 0.1 s, 0.2 s, 0.4 s … capped at 2 s."""
360	    return min(BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS * (2.0**attempt), BACKOFF_MAX_SECONDS)
361

375	def get_audit_logger() -> AuditLogger:
376	    """Get a concrete audit logger via environment variables"""

378	    if table_name is not None:

395	def get_audit_log_reader() -> FallbackAuditLogReader:
396	    """Get an audit log reader configured from environment variables.
397	
398	    Uses the DynamoDB-backed audit logger as the primary source and an S3
399	    reader as the fallback. Both must be configured via environment.
400	    """
401	    primary: AuditLogger = get_audit_logger()
402	
403	    bucket: str | None = os.getenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET)
404	    if bucket is None or bucket == "":
405	        raise ValueError(f"{AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET} was not set")
406	    prefix: str = os.getenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX, "")
407	
408	    fallback = get_s3_audit_log_reader(bucket=bucket, prefix=prefix)
409	    return FallbackAuditLogReader(primary=primary, fallback=fallback)

412	def get_s3_audit_log_writer(bucket: str, prefix: str) -> S3AuditLogWriter:
413	    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
414	    return S3AuditLogWriter(bucket=bucket, prefix=prefix, s3_client=s3)

416	
417	def get_audit_log_archiver() -> AuditArchiver:
418	    """Get an AuditArchiver configured from environment variables.
419	
420	    Requires ``DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE``, ``AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET``, and
421	    optionally ``AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX``.
422	    """
423	    table_name: str | None = os.getenv(DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE)
424	    if table_name is None:
425	        raise ValueError(f"{DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE} was not set")
426	
427	    bucket: str | None = os.getenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET)
428	    if bucket is None or bucket == "":
429	        raise ValueError(f"{AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET} was not set")
430	    prefix: str = os.getenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX, "")
431	
432	    primary = get_dynamodb_audit_logger(table_name)
433	    writer = get_s3_audit_log_writer(bucket=bucket, prefix=prefix)
434	    return AuditArchiver(primary=primary, writer=writer)

35	    PROCESS_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
36	        "new",

44	    def __init__(
45	        self,
46	        repository: RequestRepository,
47	        audit: AuditLogger,
48	        as400: AS400Notifier,
49	        customer_api: CustomerAPIClient,
50	        erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI,
51	        zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI,
52	        dry_run: bool = False,
53	        archiver: AuditArchiver | None = None,
54	    ) -> None:
55	        self.repo: RequestRepository = repository
56	        self.audit: AuditLogger = audit
57	        self.as400: AS400Notifier = as400
58	        self.dry_run: Final[bool] = dry_run
59	        self.customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = customer_api
60	        self.erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI = erasures_api
61	        self.zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI = zendesk
62	        self.archiver: AuditArchiver | None = archiver

66	    def _check_timeout(self, timeout: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
67	        if timeout is None:
68	            return False
69	        now: datetime = datetime.now()
70	        return now >= timeout
71	
72	    def process(self, timeout: datetime | None = None):
73	        """
74	        Process the requests with an optional timeout to bail early before.
75	
76	        When working in a time limited environment like AWS Lambda, this
77	        timeout should be set a little before the whole Lambda will expire to
78	        account for interrupting the running logic.
79	
80	        The only time an error should be raised related to the timeout is if we
81	        timeout before the process has even begun as this indicates too short a
82	        timeout.
83	        """
84	
85	        if timeout is not None:
86	            logger.info("Process triggered with timeout of %s", timeout.isoformat())
87	        else:
88	            logger.info("Process triggered without timeout")
89	
90	        # This should do the following:
91	        # - Get all requests in a non-terminal state
92	        # - process each request appropriatelty
93	        # It should do this for each different non-terminal state in turn and
94	        # be checking for time remaining in-between each to make sure that we
95	        # have enough time left for this invocation, since this could be running
96	        # in an AWS Lambda.
97	        # We can also happily continue at the next function call since we will
98	        # be running often
99	
100	        if self._check_timeout(timeout):
101	            # Timed out before we began
102	            # This isn't right so raise an error
103	            raise ValueError("We timed out before we began")
104	
105	        # NOTE: We could improve the timeout logic by timning each previous
106	        # process. We'd then use this data to estimate if we'd timeout rather
107	        # than using a safety margin
108	
109	        # Collect some stats on the process
110	        failed_request_count: int = 0
111	        successful_request_count: int = 0
112	        processed_request_count: int = 0
113	        timed_out: bool = False
114	
115	        # NOTE: When we get all those requests in a given state, we may
116	        # well end up processing the same request through multiple states
117	        # if it has been transitioned to the next AND the storage layer has
118	        # consistently updated.
119	        for state in self.PROCESS_STATES:
120	            # Stop early if we've timed out
121	            # We can process this state in another iteration
122	            if self._check_timeout(timeout):
123	                logger.info('Timed out before checking state "%s"', state)
124	                timed_out = True
125	                break
126	
127	            all_in_state = self.repo.get_all_in_state(state)
128	            logger.info('Found %d entries for state "%s"', len(all_in_state), state)
129	
130	            for request in all_in_state:
131	                # Stop processing the items if we've timed out
132	                # We can pick back up in the next iteration
133	                if self._check_timeout(timeout):
134	                    logger.info(
135	                        'Timed out while checking state "%s" before request: %s',
136	                        state,
137	                        request,
138	                    )
139	                    timed_out = True
140	                    break
141	                # Process the single erasure request
142	                # We aren't using batch operations here so that we can be
143	                # idempotent as it relates to each individual request
144	                processed_request_count += 1
145	                try:
146	                    # We do this in a try block as we don't want the whole
147	                    # process to fail if individual requests do
148	                    self.process_request(request)
149	                except ErasureRequestException:
150	                    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
151	                    failed_request_count += 1
152	                    continue
153	                except Exception:
154	                    # This branch will catch any bubbled up exceptions that
155	                    # have not been wrapped by domain exceptions explicitly.
156	                    # This should not really happen as we should be handling
157	                    # the possible error states
158	                    logger.exception(
159	                        "Unknown problem processing request %s", request.id
160	                    )
161	                    failed_request_count += 1
162	                    continue
163	                successful_request_count += 1
164	                self._archive_if_done(request)
165	
166	        # After all the processing has been completed we need to quickly do
167	        # alerts as we may have timed out
168	        if processed_request_count > 0:
169	            logger.info(
170	                "Processed %d/%d requests successfully",
171	                successful_request_count,
172	                processed_request_count,
173	            )
174	            if failed_request_count >= processed_request_count:
175	                # Raise an exception if all processing failed.
176	                # This could be due to some misconfiguration so the calling
177	                # code should be alerted
178	                raise AllProcessingFailed(failed_request_count)
179	            if timed_out:
180	                # This logging message is partly redundent but if we often
181	                # time out it might be a bad sign
182	                logger.warning("Timed out while processing")
183	            if failed_request_count > 0:
184	                logger.error("%d requests failed to process", failed_request_count)
185	        else:
186	            logger.info("No requests processed")
187	            if timed_out:
188	                logger.warning("We timed out while checking states without finding any")

190	    def process_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
191	        """Process a single request"""
192	        # This is where the meat of the business logic should be
193	        logger.info("Processing request: %s", request)
194	        self.audit.log(request.id, "Request was selected for processing")
195	
196	        # Defensively align the state to a standard lower case format
197	        request_state: str = request.state.strip().lower()
198	        if request_state not in VALID_STATES:
199	            raise InvalidRequestState(request)
200	
201	        cast_request_state: ErasureState = request_state
202	        match cast_request_state:
203	            case "new":
204	                self.process_new(request)
205	            case "in-progress":
206	                self.process_in_progress(request)
207	            case "phase-1":
208	                self.process_phase1(request)
209	            case "phase-2":
210	                self.process_phase2(request)
211	            case "phase-3":
212	                self.process_phase3(request)
213	            case _:
214	                raise NotImplementedError(
215	                    f'Request processing of state "{request_state}" not implemented'
216	                )
217	
218	    def _archive_if_done(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
219	        """Archive the audit log to S3 if the request has reached a terminal "done" state.
220	
221	        Precondition: ``process_request`` must have been the call that
222	        transitioned ``request.state`` to ``"done"`` in this same iteration.
223	        If a future handler transitions to ``"done"`` outside of
224	        ``process_request``, it must trigger archival separately.
225	
226	        Archive failures are swallowed and logged so they do not fail the
227	        surrounding ``process`` sweep — the next invocation will retry from
228	        DynamoDB.
229	
230	        NOTE: No production handler currently reaches ``"done"`` (phase-2 and
231	        phase-3 raise ``NotImplementedError``). This method will become live
232	        once the phase-3 handler is implemented and ends with a transition to
233	        ``"done"``.
234	        """
235	        if request.state != "done":
236	            return
237	        if self.dry_run:
238	            logger.info("DryRun: would archive audit log for %s", request.id)
239	            return
240	        if self.archiver is None:
241	            return
242	        try:
243	            self.archiver.archive(request.id)
244	        except Exception:
245	            logger.exception(
246	                "Failed to archive audit log for request %s; will be retried on next invocation",
247	                request.id,
248	            )

250	    @classmethod
251	    def _validate_request(
252	        cls, current_state: ErasureState, request: ErasureRequest
253	    ) -> None:
254	        """
255	        Validate a request for the given current state.
256	
257	        This will raise an exception if the request is not valid.
258	        """
259	        if request.state != current_state:
260	            raise WrongStateForProcess(request, current_state)

262	    def _transition_request(
263	        self,
264	        request: ErasureRequest,
265	        new_state: ErasureState,
266	        reason: str | None = None,
267	    ) -> None:
268	        """
269	        Helper function to transition a request to a new state.
270	
271	        You can provide an optional reason that will be used in audit logging.
272	        """
273	        logger.info("Transitioning request %s to %s", request.id, new_state)
274	        request.state = new_state
275	        if self.dry_run:
276	            logger.info("DryRun: Would update repository")
277	        else:
278	            self.repo.update(request)
279	        message = f"Request was transitioned to {new_state}"
280	        if reason:
281	            message = message + " due to: " + reason
282	        if not self.dry_run:
283	            self.audit.log(request.id, message)

337	    def process_phase2(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
338	        # This is where the core of erasure logic happened in Tines
339	        self._validate_request("phase-2", request)
340	
341	        # TODO: Update OneTrust throughout each part of phase-2
342	
343	        # Erasure Service API
344	        # If not present we will submit to the API otherwise we will check
345	        # status
346	        if request.erasure_service_id is None:
347	            # Submit an erasure to the Customer Erasures API
348	            if self.dry_run:
349	                logger.info(
350	                    "Would have submitted erasure request to API for %s", request.id
351	                )
352	            else:
353	                self.submit_erasure_to_apis(request)
354	            return
355	
356	        try:
357	            erasure_status = self.erasures_api.check_erasure(request)
358	        except CustomerErasureAPIException as e:
359	            raise ProblemWithErasuresAPI(
360	                request, "Problem checking erasures API"
361	            ) from e
362	        # TODO: What on earth is considered "done" for erasures?
363	        # The Tines story seems to just count successes and update Jira
364	        # conditionally.
365	        # https://hnb.tines.com/stories/28027?actions=541831&event=8781951946
366	
367	        # {
368	        # "tasksuccesscount": ?,
369	        # "DWHtasksuccesscount": ?,
370	        # "tasknotfoundcount": ?
371	        # }
372	        # It checks if tasksuccesscount > 0 and does something if
373	        # tasknotfoundcount > 0 too
374	        # Then it waits for DWHtasksuccesscount > 0 too?
375	
376	        task_success_count: int = 0
377	        dwh_task_success_count: int = 0
378	        task_not_found_count: int = 0
379	        for key, task in erasure_status.tasks.items():
380	            if task.status == "SUCCESS":
381	                if key == "DELETE_DWH_DATA":
382	                    dwh_task_success_count += 1
383	                else:
384	                    task_success_count += 1
385	            if (
386	                task.error_message == "Customer not found on customer-api"
387	                or task.error_message == "NO Customers Found"
388	            ):
389	                task_not_found_count += 1
390	
391	        if task_success_count > 0 and dwh_task_success_count > 0:
392	            # We can repeat the Zendesk search and deletes because they should no
393	            # longer turn up after the first amount
394	            try:
395	                # Subflow 3 from Tines:
396	                # Search and Delete Zendesk Tickets
397	                found_tickets = self.delete_zendesk_tickets(request)
398	
399	                # Subflow 4 from Tines:
400	                # Search and delete Zendesk users
401	                found_users = self.delete_zendesk_users(request)
402	
403	                # Subflow 5 from Tines:
404	                # Purge the deleted Zendesk tickets
405	                self.purge_zendesk_tickets(found_tickets)
406	
407	                # Subflow 6 from Tines:
408	                # Purge the deleted Zendesk users
409	                self.purge_zendesk_users(found_users)
410	                zendesk_steps_succeeded = True
411	            except Exception as e:
412	                # Treat Zendesk failures as impediments to success
413	                zendesk_steps_succeeded = False
414	                logger.exception("Failure communicating with Zendesk")
415	                self.audit.log(
416	                    request.id, f"Issue in communicating with Zendesk {str(e)}"
417	                )
418	                # If wanted we can transition to a failure state to stop retries
419	
420	            if zendesk_steps_succeeded:
421	                # Finally after all this we transition to the next stage
422	                self._transition_request(request, "phase-3")

424	    def delete_zendesk_tickets(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> list[ZendeskTicketId]:
425	        found_tickets = self.zendesk.find_tickets(request)
426	        ticket_count = len(found_tickets)
427	        if ticket_count > 0:
428	            tickets_string = ",".join(found_tickets)
429	            if self.dry_run:
430	                logger.info(
431	                    "Would submit %d deletes to Zendesk for tickets: %s",
432	                    ticket_count,
433	                    tickets_string,
434	                )
435	            else:
436	                self.audit.log(
437	                    request.id,
438	                    f"Deleting {ticket_count} Zendesk Tickets: {tickets_string}",
439	                )
440	                self.zendesk.delete_tickets(found_tickets)
441	        else:
442	            if not self.dry_run:
443	                self.audit.log(request.id, "Found no Zendesk Tickets")
444	        return found_tickets

447	        found_users = self.zendesk.find_users(request)
448	        user_count = len(found_users)
449	        if user_count > 0:

458	                for user in found_users:

461	            if not self.dry_run:

479	    def process_phase3(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
480	        self._validate_request("phase-3", request)
481	        # Phase-3 from Tines
482	        # This is just checking if the customer still appears in the customer
483	        # API or not
484	        if "email" not in request.erasure_details:
485	            raise MissingEmailAddress(request)
486	        email = request.erasure_details["email"]
487	        found = self.customer_api.get_customer_details(email)
488	        if len(found) > 0:
489	            # TODO: What should we do if the previous erasure states all failed?
490	            self._transition_request(
491	                request,
492	                "need-more-detail",
493	                "Customer email still present in Customer API",
494	            )
495	            raise StillExistsAfterErasure(request)
496	
497	        self._transition_request(request, "done")

499	    def enrich_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
500	        """Enrich the erasure request if needed with details from other sources"""
501	        # Enrichment could include getting more details on the customer that
502	        # will be needed by downstream erasure processes
503	
504	        if "email" not in request.erasure_details:
505	            raise MissingEmailAddress(request)
506	
507	        # We will try and find details from all customer regions
508	        enrichment_data: list[CustomerDetails] = []
509	        enrichment_data.extend(
510	            self.customer_api.get_customer_details(
511	                email=request.erasure_details["email"]
512	            )
513	        )
514	        for site_id in ALL_SITE_IDS:
515	            enrichment_data.extend(
516	                self.customer_api.get_customer_details(
517	                    email=request.erasure_details["email"], site_id=site_id
518	                )
519	            )
520	
521	        found_site_ids: set[int] = set()
522	        fields_added: list[str] = []
523	        for entry in enrichment_data:
524	            # Enrich with the first found entries
525	            # If the customer has used the email in multiple regions with
526	            # different details this might enrich with weird values.
527	            # Essentially this is a best effort enrichment as email should be
528	            # enough for most systems to erase by, the extra details are just
529	            # useful for debugging purposes.
530	            if entry.first_name and "first-name" not in request.erasure_details:
531	                request.erasure_details["first-name"] = entry.first_name
532	                fields_added.append("first-name")
533	            if entry.last_name and "last-name" not in request.erasure_details:
534	                request.erasure_details["last-name"] = entry.last_name
535	                fields_added.append("last-name")
536	            if entry.loyalty_number and "rfl" not in request.erasure_details:
537	                request.erasure_details["rfl"] = entry.loyalty_number
538	                fields_added.append("rfl")
539	            if entry.mobile_number and "phone-number" not in request.erasure_details:
540	                request.erasure_details["phone-number"] = entry.mobile_number
541	                fields_added.append("phone-number")
542	            if entry.site_id:
543	                found_site_ids.add(entry.site_id)
544	
545	        audit_message: str | None = None
546	        if len(fields_added) > 0:
547	            fields_list = ",".join(fields_added)
548	            audit_message = (
549	                f"The following fields were added via enrichment: {fields_list}"
550	            )
551	        # Update site_ids
552	        current_site_ids = set(request.site_ids)
553	        if current_site_ids != found_site_ids:
554	            new_site_ids: list[int] = list(found_site_ids | current_site_ids)
555	            new_site_ids.sort()
556	            if new_site_ids != request.site_ids:
557	                request.site_ids = new_site_ids
558	                new_sites_list: str = ",".join(map(str, new_site_ids))
559	                sites_updated_msg = f"site_ids were updated to: {new_sites_list}"
560	                if audit_message is None:
561	                    audit_message = sites_updated_msg
562	                else:
563	                    audit_message += f"\n{sites_updated_msg}"
564	
565	        # Update audit log with details on enrichment
566	        if audit_message is not None:
567	            self.audit.log(request.id, audit_message)
568	
569	    def submit_erasure_to_apis(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
570	        """
571	        Submit the erasure request to the Erasures API.
572	
573	        This function will transition the request on failures appropriately.
574	        """
575	        email = request.erasure_details.get("email")
576	        if email is None:
577	            # This is unlikely to happen unless the request has skipped some
578	            # steps
579	            logger.error("Request %s has no email", request.id)
580	            self._transition_request(request, "need-more-detail", "Missing email")
581	            return None
582	
583	        try:
584	            erasures_id = self.erasures_api.trigger_erasure(request)
585	        except CustomerErasureAPIException as e:
586	            raise ProblemWithErasuresAPI(
587	                request, "Problem triggering erasures API"
588	            ) from e

591	            f"Sent request to erasures API service and got id back: {erasures_id}",
592	        )
593	        request.erasure_service_id = erasures_id
594	        self.repo.update(request)

43	def _require_str(body: dict[str, object], key: str) -> str:
44	    v = body[key]
45	    if not isinstance(v, str) or not v:
46	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Field '{key}' must be a non-empty string")
47	    if len(v) > _MAX_FIELD_LEN:
48	        raise InvalidRequestError(
49	            f"Field '{key}' must be <= {_MAX_FIELD_LEN} characters"
50	        )
51	    return v

54	def request_from_event(
55	    event: dict[str, object],
56	) -> ErasureRequest:
57	    """Convert the input event into an ErasureRequest"""
58	    try:
59	        raw_body: str = cast(str, event.get("body") or "")
60	        if event.get("isBase64Encoded"):
61	            raw_body = base64.b64decode(raw_body).decode("utf-8")
62	        body: dict[str, object] = cast(dict[str, object], json.loads(raw_body))
63	    except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError) as e:
64	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Invalid JSON body: {e}") from e
65	
66	    required_fields = ("id", "source", "date_issued", "erasure_details")
67	    missing = [f for f in required_fields if f not in body]
68	    if missing:
69	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Missing required fields: {', '.join(missing)}")
70	
71	    state_raw = body.get("state", "new")
72	    if not isinstance(state_raw, str):
73	        raise InvalidRequestError("Field 'state' must be a string")
74	    if state_raw != "new":
75	        raise InvalidRequestError(
76	            f"Erasure requests must be created in state 'new', got '{state_raw}'"
77	        )
78	
79	    date_issued_raw = body["date_issued"]
80	    if not isinstance(date_issued_raw, str):
81	        raise InvalidRequestError("Field 'date_issued' must be a string")
82	    try:
83	        date_issued = datetime.fromisoformat(date_issued_raw)
84	    except ValueError as e:
85	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Invalid date_issued: {e}") from e
86	
87	    id_ = _require_str(body, "id")
88	    source = _require_str(body, "source")
89	
90	    erasure_details_raw = body["erasure_details"]
91	    if not isinstance(erasure_details_raw, dict):
92	        raise InvalidRequestError("erasure_details must be an object")
93	    erasure_details: dict[ErasureDetailKey, str] = {}
94	    for k, v in cast(dict[str, object], erasure_details_raw).items():
95	        if k not in VALID_DETAIL_KEYS:
96	            raise InvalidRequestError(f"Unknown erasure_details key: '{k}'")
97	        if not isinstance(v, str) or not v:
98	            raise InvalidRequestError(
99	                f"erasure_details['{k}'] must be a non-empty string"
100	            )
101	        erasure_details[k] = v  # type: ignore[literal-required]
102	
103	    site_ids: list[int] = []
104	    if "site_ids" in body:
105	        if body["site_ids"] is not None:
106	            if not isinstance(body["site_ids"], list):
107	                raise InvalidRequestError("site_ids must be a list")
108	            site_ids_list = cast(list[object], body["site_ids"])
109	            for item in site_ids_list:
110	                if type(item) is not int:
111	                    raise InvalidRequestError("site_ids entries must be integers")
112	                site_ids.append(item)
113	
114	    erasure_service_id: str | None = None
115	    if "erasure_service_id" in body:
116	        raw_value_erasure_service_id = body["erasure_service_id"]
117	        if raw_value_erasure_service_id is not None:
118	            if not isinstance(raw_value_erasure_service_id, str):
119	                raise InvalidRequestError("erasure_service_id must be a string or null")
120	            erasure_service_id = raw_value_erasure_service_id
121	
122	    return ErasureRequest(
123	        id=id_,
124	        source=source,
125	        state=state_raw,
126	        date_issued=date_issued,
127	        erasure_details=erasure_details,
128	        site_ids=site_ids,
129	        erasure_service_id=erasure_service_id,
130	    )

133	def _response(
134	    status_code: int,
135	    body: dict[str, str | int],
136	) -> dict[str, object]:
137	    return {
138	        "statusCode": status_code,
139	        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
140	        "body": json.dumps(body),

147	    event: dict[str, object],
148	    _context: LambdaContext,
149	) -> dict[str, object]:
150	    """
151	    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does item creation.
152	
153	    This should be very simple, the Lambda should simply create an entry in the
154	    underlying storage, another lambda or piece of compute will process them.
155	    """
156	    try:
157	        request: ErasureRequest = request_from_event(event)
158	    except (InvalidRequestError, json.JSONDecodeError) as e:
159	        logger.warning("Rejecting erasure request with invalid body: %s", str(e))
160	        return _response(400, {"error": "invalid request body"})

162	    try:

33	class RequestRepository(ABC):
34	    """Common interface for dealing with Erasure Request storage"""
35	
36	    @abstractmethod
37	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
38	        """Create a new erasure request in the underlying storage"""
39	        raise NotImplementedError()
40	
41	    @abstractmethod
42	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
43	        """
44	        Updates an existing item in the underlying storage based on the given request.
45	
46	        Note that the requests id is used to do the updating.
47	        """
48	        raise NotImplementedError()

50	    @abstractmethod
51	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
52	        """Get a specific request from the underlying storage based upon its id."""
53	        raise NotImplementedError()

55	    @abstractmethod
56	    def get_all_in_state(
57	        self,
58	        state: ErasureState,
59	        limit: int = 100,
60	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
61	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
62	        """
63	        Get the erasure requests within a given state up to a certain limit.
64	
65	        When the limit is reached, that many items are returned. There could be
66	        more items with the given state.
67	
68	        You can optionally set a from_date to filter only results from that
69	        given time and date until now.
70	
71	        Warning: This can return a lot of results if you use a terminal state.
72	
73	        Warning: Implementations may cap the amount of items returned under the
74	        given limit if certain conditions are met. For example, DynamoDB will
75	        stop returning items for a single query if the total size reaches 1 MB.
76	        It is up to implementations as to how much they respect this limit.
77	        For example, a DynamoDB implementation could paginate.
78	
79	        """
80	        raise NotImplementedError()

83	class InMemoryRequestRepository(RequestRepository):
84	    """
85	    An example implementation of a request repository.
86	
87	    This implementation simply stores objects in an internal dictionary.
88	    It does not clone objects.
89	
90	    This implementation is not thread safe.
91	    """
92	
93	    def __init__(self) -> None:
94	        super().__init__()
95	        self._db: dict[str, ErasureRequest] = {}
96	
97	    @override
98	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
99	        if request.id in self._db:
100	            raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id)
101	        self._db[request.id] = request
102	
103	    @override
104	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
105	        if request.id not in self._db:
106	            raise ValueError(f"{request.id} does not already exists")
107	        self._db[request.id] = request
108	
109	    @override
110	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
111	        return self._db.get(id)
112	
113	    @override
114	    def get_all_in_state(
115	        self, state: ErasureState, limit: int = 100, from_date: datetime | None = None
116	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
117	        if limit <= 0:
118	            return []
119	        found: list[ErasureRequest] = []
120	        count: int = 0
121	        for _, item in self._db.items():
122	            if item.state == state:
123	                if from_date is not None and item.date_issued < from_date:
124	                    continue
125	                found.append(item)
126	                count += 1
127	                if count >= limit:
128	                    break
129	        return found

132	class DynamoDBRequestRepository(RequestRepository):

135	    ID_FIELD: Literal["id"] = "id"
136	    STATE_FIELD: Literal["state"] = "state"
137	    DATE_ISSUED_FIELD: Literal["date_issued"] = "date_issued"
138	    SOURCE_FIELD: Literal["source"] = "source"
139	    ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD: Literal["erasure_details"] = "erasure_details"
140	    SITE_IDS_FIELD: Literal["site_ids"] = "site_ids"
141	    ERASURE_SERVICE_ID_FIELD: Literal["erasure_service_id"] = "erasure_service_id"
142	
143	    NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_not_exists({ID_FIELD})"
144	    EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_exists({ID_FIELD})"
145	
146	    def __init__(
147	        self, table_name: str, index_name: str, dynamodb: DynamoDBClient
148	    ) -> None:
149	        super().__init__()
150	        self.table_name: str = table_name
151	        self.index_name: str = index_name
152	        self.dynamodb: DynamoDBClient = dynamodb
153	
154	    @override
155	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
156	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
157	        try:
158	            # This should error if there is an item in the table with the given id
159	            _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
160	                TableName=self.table_name,
161	                Item=item,
162	                ReturnValues="NONE",
163	                ConditionExpression=self.NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION,
164	            )
165	        except ClientError as e:
166	            if (
167	                e.response.get("Error", {}).get("Code")
168	                == "ConditionalCheckFailedException"
169	            ):
170	                raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id) from e
171	            raise
172	
173	    @override
174	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
175	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
176	        # This should error if there is NOT an item in the table with the given
177	        # id
178	        _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
179	            TableName=self.table_name,
180	            Item=item,
181	            ReturnValues="NONE",
182	            ConditionExpression=self.EXISTS_CONDITION,
183	        )
184	
185	    @override
186	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
187	        response = self.dynamodb.get_item(
188	            TableName=self.table_name, Key={self.ID_FIELD: {"S": id}}
189	        )
190	
191	        if "Item" in response:
192	            item: dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef] = response["Item"]
193	            # Convert the item back to a domain object
194	            return self.response_to_request(item)
195	        else:
196	            return None
197	
198	    @override
199	    def get_all_in_state(
200	        self,
201	        state: ErasureState,
202	        limit: int = 100,
203	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
204	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
205	        if limit <= 0:
206	            return []
207	        key_condition: str = "#S = :state"
208	
209	        #  The attribute values being used in the query
210	        attribute_values: Mapping[str, UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef] = {
211	            ":state": {"S": state}
212	        }
213	        # Replacements for attribute names that are reserved keywords
214	        attribute_names: Mapping[str, str] = {"#S": "state"}
215	
216	        if from_date is not None:
217	            # Add on the sort key condition to the key_condition
218	            key_condition += " AND date_issued >= :date"
219	            # Add the sort key value to attribute_values
220	            timestamp = int(from_date.timestamp() * 1000)
221	            attribute_values[":date"] = {"N": str(timestamp)}
222	
223	        paginator = self.dynamodb.get_paginator("query")
224	        pages = paginator.paginate(
225	            TableName=self.table_name,
226	            IndexName=self.index_name,
227	            Select="ALL_ATTRIBUTES",
228	            ScanIndexForward=True,  # explicitly set direction of scan
229	            KeyConditionExpression=key_condition,
230	            ExpressionAttributeNames=attribute_names,
231	            ExpressionAttributeValues=attribute_values,
232	            PaginationConfig={"PageSize": min([100, limit]), "MaxItems": limit},
233	        )
234	
235	        results: list[ErasureRequest] = []
236	        for page in pages:
237	            # iterate pages and add converted results to list
238	            if "Items" not in page:
239	                continue
240	            for item in page["Items"]:
241	                erasure = self.response_to_request(item)
242	                results.append(erasure)
243	
244	        return results

246	    @classmethod
247	    def response_to_request(
248	        cls, response: dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef]
249	    ) -> ErasureRequest:
250	        """Convert a response dictionary to an ErasureRequest"""
251	        if cls.ID_FIELD not in response:
252	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.ID_FIELD} field")
253	        if cls.STATE_FIELD not in response:
254	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.STATE_FIELD} field")
255	        if cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD not in response:
256	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD} field")
257	        if cls.SOURCE_FIELD not in response:
258	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.SOURCE_FIELD} field")
259	
260	        id: str = response[cls.ID_FIELD][
261	            "S"
262	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
263	        state: str = response[cls.STATE_FIELD][
264	            "S"
265	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
266	        source: str = response[cls.SOURCE_FIELD][
267	            "S"
268	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
269	
270	        date_issued_field = response[cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD]
271	        if "N" not in date_issued_field:
272	            raise ValueError(f"Wrong type for {cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD}")
273	        date_issued_ms = int(date_issued_field["N"])
274	        date_issued: datetime = datetime.fromtimestamp(date_issued_ms / 1000)
275	
276	        erasure_details: dict[str, str] = {}
277	        if cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD in response:
278	            erasure_details_field = response[cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD]
279	            if "M" in erasure_details_field:
280	                returned_map = erasure_details_field["M"]
281	                for k, v in returned_map.items():  # pyright: ignore[reportAny]
282	                    coerced = cast(dict[str, str], v)
283	                    value: str = coerced["S"]
284	                    erasure_details[k] = value
285	
286	        site_ids: list[int] = []
287	        if cls.SITE_IDS_FIELD in response:
288	            site_ids_field = response[cls.SITE_IDS_FIELD]
289	            if "L" in site_ids_field:
290	                returned_list: Sequence[AttributeValueTypeDef] = site_ids_field["L"]
291	                for item in returned_list:
292	                    if "N" in item:
293	                        site_id = int(item["N"])
294	                        site_ids.append(site_id)
295	                    else:
296	                        raise ValueError(
297	                            f"Wrong type inside {cls.SITE_IDS_FIELD} list: {item}"
298	                        )
299	
300	        erasure_service_id: str | None = None
301	
302	        if cls.ERASURE_SERVICE_ID_FIELD in response:
303	            erasure_service_id = response[cls.ERASURE_SERVICE_ID_FIELD][
304	                "S"
305	            ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
306	
307	        # TODO: Should we validate the fields afterward?
308	        return ErasureRequest(
309	            id=id,
310	            state=state,  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]
311	            date_issued=date_issued,
312	            source=source,
313	            erasure_details=erasure_details,  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType],
314	            site_ids=site_ids,
315	            erasure_service_id=erasure_service_id,
316	        )
317	
318	    @classmethod
319	    def item_from_request(cls, request: ErasureRequest):
320	        """Convert a request to a DynamoDB Item"""
321	        # Build the map type
322	        erasure_details: dict[str, dict[str, str]] = {}
323	        for k, v in request.erasure_details.items():
324	            erasure_details[k] = {"S": v}
325	
326	        item: dict[
327	            str, dict[str, str | dict[str, dict[str, str]] | list[dict[str, str]]]
328	        ] = {
329	            cls.ID_FIELD: {"S": request.id},
330	            cls.STATE_FIELD: {"S": request.state.strip().lower()},
331	            cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD: {"N": str(request.date_issued_ms)},
332	            cls.SOURCE_FIELD: {"S": request.source},
333	            cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD: {"M": erasure_details},
334	        }
335	        if len(request.site_ids) > 0:
336	            # Build site_ids list
337	            site_ids_list: list[dict[str, str]] = []
338	            for site_id in request.site_ids:
339	                site_ids_list.append({"N": str(site_id)})
340	            item[cls.SITE_IDS_FIELD] = {"L": site_ids_list}
341	        if request.erasure_service_id is not None:
342	            item[cls.ERASURE_SERVICE_ID_FIELD] = {"S": request.erasure_service_id}
343	        return item

347	"""
348	Environment variable used for defining the DynamoDB table used when creating
349	from the environment.
350	
351	When testing this variable should not be favoured, instead you should set the
352	table name explicitly.
353	"""
354	
355	DYNAMO_DB_INDEX_ENV_VAR: Literal["DYNAMO_DB_INDEX"] = "DYNAMO_DB_INDEX"
356	"""
357	Environment variable used for defining the DynamoDB index used when creating
358	from the environment.
359	
360	When testing this variable should not be favoured, instead you should set the
361	index name explicitly.
362	"""
363	
364	
365	def get_dyanmodb_request_repository() -> DynamoDBRequestRepository:
366	    """Return a DynamoDB request repository from using environment variables"""
367	
368	    table_name: str | None = os.getenv(DYNAMO_DB_TABLE_ENV_VAR)
369	    if table_name is None or table_name == "":
370	        raise ValueError(f"{DYNAMO_DB_TABLE_ENV_VAR} was not set")
371	
372	    index_name: str | None = os.getenv(DYNAMO_DB_INDEX_ENV_VAR)
373	    if index_name is None or index_name == "":
374	        raise ValueError(f"{DYNAMO_DB_INDEX_ENV_VAR} was not set")
375	
376	    dynamodb: DynamoDBClient = boto3.client("dynamodb")
377	    return DynamoDBRequestRepository(
378	        table_name=table_name, index_name=index_name, dynamodb=dynamodb
379	    )
380	
381	
382	REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR: Literal["REPOSITORY_TYPE"] = "REPOSITORY_TYPE"
383	"""The environment variable used to force a specific repository type"""
384	
385	
386	def get_request_repository() -> RequestRepository:
387	    """Get a concrete repository via the environment variables"""
388	    repo_type: str | None = os.getenv(REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR)
389	
390	    match repo_type:
391	        case "" | None:
392	            return _get_default_request_repository()
393	        case s:
394	            match s.strip().lower():
395	                case "dynamodb":
396	                    return get_dyanmodb_request_repository()
397	                case "memory":
398	                    return InMemoryRequestRepository()
399	                case "":
400	                    return _get_default_request_repository()
401	                case _:
402	                    raise ValueError(f"Unknown {REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR} entry: {s}")
403	
404	
405	def _get_default_request_repository() -> RequestRepository:
406	    """Return a default request repository, this is currently DynamoDB"""
407	    return get_dyanmodb_request_repository()

@@ -28,6 +28,34 @@ The orchestrator exposes a private REST API (deployed via AWS API Gateway and
 restricted to the configured VPC endpoint). All requests must include the

+### `POST /erasure-request`
+
+Creates a new erasure request and queues it for processing.
+
+```sh
+curl -k -sS -X POST \
+  'https://<api-id>.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/erasure-request' \
+  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>' \
+  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
+  -d '{
+    "id": "<unique-id>",
+    "source": "onetrust",
+    "date_issued": "2026-01-01T12:00:00+00:00",
+    "erasure_details": { "email": "user@example.com" }
+  }'
+```
+
+Required fields: `id`, `source`, `date_issued` (ISO-8601), `erasure_details`
+(object with keys from `email`, `first-name`, `last-name`, `postcode`,
+`phone-number`, `rfl`, `last-order`). Optional: `site_ids` (list of integers).
+
+| Status | Meaning |
+|--------|---------|
+| `200` | Request accepted; body is `{"id": "<id>"}` |
+| `400` | Invalid or malformed request body |
+| `409` | A request with this `id` already exists |
+| `500` | Internal error |
+
 ### `GET /status/{id}`
 
 Returns the audit log entries for an erasure request, ordered chronologically
@@ -54,6 +82,19 @@ A `200` response has the form:
 The endpoint returns `404` if no audit entries exist for the given id, and

1	---
2	name: serverless-access-manager review playbook
3	description: Review rules for SAM templates using or eligible for the team's Custom::ServerlessAccessManager Lambda-backed custom resource
4	type: reference
5	---
6	
7	# AWS SAM: serverless-access-manager Custom Resource — Review Playbook
8	
9	When reviewing SAM templates, check whether the team's internal `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` Lambda-backed custom resource is being used correctly. This resource is the canonical pattern for managing Lambda execution roles, VPC security groups, and Datadog log forwarding — defined at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md` (re-read this file before finalising findings; the source/target list can drift).
10	
11	**Before producing findings, re-read `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md` to get the current canonical source/target type list.**
12	
13	## Recommend adoption when serverless-access-manager is absent
14	
15	Flag as **Medium** any SAM template that manually defines an `AWS::IAM::Role` execution role for a Lambda that interacts with services covered by a supported source or target type (DynamoDB, MSK, S3, SQS, SNS, Kinesis, EventBridge, ElasticSearch, SSM, Connect, Lex, LambdaFunctionUrl, Transcribe). These templates should use `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` instead to reduce boilerplate and keep IAM management centralised.
16	
17	## Required properties and correct `!GetAtt` wiring
18	
19	For any template that already uses `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager`:
20	
21	- `FunctionName` must be set on the custom resource — it is the key used for naming all underlying resources.
22	- Lambda `Role` **must** reference `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn`. An inline or separately-declared IAM role for the same function is a **High** issue — the custom resource manages the role; a second role is unused or conflicts.
23	- When VPC execution is enabled (explicitly or implicitly via ElasticSearch/MSK sources or targets), Lambda `VpcConfig` **must** be `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig`. Hardcoding subnet/SG IDs is **Medium**.
24	- Lambda `FunctionName` should reuse `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName` (deduplicates the string). Using a literal string that duplicates the `FunctionName` property value is **Low**.
25	
26	## `Type` value validation (case-sensitive)
27	
28	All `Type` values in `Sources` and `Targets` are case-sensitive. Flag any mis-cased value as **High** — an incorrect type is silently ignored or causes `InvalidInput`.
29	
30	Valid source types: `Connect`, `DynamoDB`, `ElasticSearch`, `Kinesis`, `Lex`, `MSK`, `S3`, `SNS`, `SQS`, `SSM`, `Transcribe`.
31	
32	Valid target types: `Connect`, `DynamoDB`, `ElasticSearch`, `EventBridge`, `LambdaFunctionUrl`, `MSK`, `S3`, `SNS`, `SQS`, `Transcribe`.
33	
34	Common mistake: `Eventbridge` → must be `EventBridge`.
35	
36	## Stack-tag requirements
37	
38	The custom resource validates stack tags before provisioning. Required tags must be present and **all tag keys and values must be lowercase**. Violation causes an `InvalidInput` error at deploy time — flag as **High**. Example: `Owner: MyTeam` must be `owner: myteam`.
39	
40	## SSM source path-prefix restriction
41	
42	The `SSM` source type only supports these parameter path prefixes: `/corp-sys/`, `/sc-spange/`, `/dns/`, `/datascience/`. Flag any `Parameters` entry outside these prefixes as **High** — it will fail at runtime.
43	
44	## Transcribe source/target specifics
45	
46	- `input_prefix` and `output_prefix` must use wildcards that match the actual S3 path layout (e.g. `*/concatenated/composited-video/*.mp4`).
47	- `output_prefix` must cover the entire output folder, not just `*.json`. AWS Transcribe writes `.write_access_check_file.temp` to the output prefix before starting the job — a narrowly-scoped prefix causes a silent failure. Flag `output_prefix` patterns scoped only to `*.json` as **High**.
48	- If the Lambda runs in a private subnet and the template does not include evidence of a NAT Gateway or Transcribe PrivateLink, flag as **Medium** (AWS Transcribe is a public endpoint; a VPC-isolated Lambda will timeout silently).
49	
50	## Cross-account Kinesis source
51	
52	When a `Kinesis` source `Stream` value is a full ARN (cross-account), flag as **Info** if either of these is missing from the template:
53	
54	- An `AWS::Lambda::Permission` resource granting `kinesis.amazonaws.com` `lambda:InvokeFunction` rights with both `SourceAccount` and `SourceArn` set.
55	- A note that the stream's resource policy in the infrastructure repo must grant the execution role read permissions. (This lives outside the SAM template — flag as Info rather than High, but make the omission explicit.)
56	
57	For same-account Kinesis (`Stream` is a plain name, not an ARN), neither of these is required.
58	
59	## Redundant `VpcExecution: true`
60	
61	`VpcExecution: true` is implicitly forced by `ElasticSearch` or `MSK` sources/targets. Flag it as **Low** when the template already declares one of those — it is harmless but redundant noise.
62	
63	## Unexplained `DatadogLogging: "false"`
64	
65	`DatadogLogging` defaults to `"true"`. If a template sets it to `"false"` with no comment explaining why, flag as **Medium** — Datadog log forwarding is expected in all environments, and a silent opt-out will cause an observability gap.
66	
67	## No inline IAM alongside `serverless-access-manager`
68	
69	If the template contains `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` and also attaches an `AWS::IAM::ManagedPolicy` or inline `Policies:` to the same Lambda's execution role, flag as **High**. The custom resource owns the role; any external mutation conflicts with its reconciliation loop.
70

The DATA-4715 branch hand-rolled an `AWS::IAM::Policy` attached to the access-manager-managed role instead of using a `Targets: { ...: { Type: S3 } }` entry. I have all the context needed. Here is the review.

---

# Code Review: DATA-4715 Audit Log Archival to S3

**Scope reviewed**: the 12 DATA-4715 files named in the request, against `origin/master...HEAD`. Master-side Phase-2/Phase-3 work was excluded.

**Assumptions stated explicitly**:
- The process Lambda runs single-threaded (`ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 1`), so the S3-write-then-DynamoDB-delete sequence has no concurrent-archival race. Confirmed in `template.yaml:181`.
- `_archive_if_done` is currently dormant in production (no handler reaches `"done"` — phase-2/phase-3 are incomplete on this branch's own code), per the docstring at `request_processor.py:230-233`. Findings about it are forward-looking but the code path is exercised by tests.
- The `S3AuditLogReader`/`FallbackAuditLogReader` classes pre-exist; only the writer/archiver/delete side is new.

**Verification run**: `uv run pytest -q` → all pass (208 tests). `uv run basedpyright` → 0 errors, 0 warnings, 0 notes. Formatting/type tooling is clean, so no findings in those categories.

---

## Critical & High

### Issue 1: Inline `AWS::IAM::Policy` attached to a serverless-access-manager-managed role
**Severity**: High
**Category**: Security / Best Practice (SAM conventions)
**File**: `template.yaml:204-215` (`ProcessS3WritePolicy`)

**Explanation**: `ProcessS3WritePolicy` is a new `AWS::IAM::Policy` whose `Roles:` list points at `!GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleName` — a role owned and reconciled by the `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` custom resource. The custom resource owns that role's policy set; attaching an external `AWS::IAM::Policy` to it conflicts with its reconciliation loop and can be silently reverted, or cause drift between deploys. The canonical pattern (confirmed in `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md:171-184`) is to declare S3 access as a **Target**, which grants readwrite on the bucket:

```yaml
| S3 | `Bucket` | Name of S3 bucket to get readwrite access to. |
```

Note: the two pre-existing inline policies (`ProcessSecretsSSMPermsPolicy`, `ReporterS3ReadPolicy`) are on master and out of scope — but they show the team has already been doing this, so this is a repo-wide pattern worth correcting. The *new* `ProcessS3WritePolicy` is the in-scope one.

**Current Code**:
```yaml
ProcessS3WritePolicy:
  Type: AWS::IAM::Policy
  Properties:
    PolicyName: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-process-s3-write-${Environment}"
    PolicyDocument:
      Version: "2012-10-17"
      Statement:
        - Effect: Allow
          Action: s3:PutObject
          Resource: !Sub "arn:aws:s3:::${AuditLogS3Bucket}/${AuditLogS3Prefix}/*"
    Roles:
      - !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleName
```

**Improved Version** — add an S3 target to `ProcessServerlessAccessManager` and delete the standalone policy:
```yaml
ProcessServerlessAccessManager:
  Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
  Properties:
    # ... existing properties ...
    Targets:
      DynamoDBTable: { ... }
      AuditLogDynamoDBTable: { ... }
      DynamoDBIndex: { ... }
      AuditLogBucket:
        Type: S3
        Properties:
          Bucket: !Ref AuditLogS3Bucket
```

**Tradeoff to flag to the team**: the access-manager S3 Target grants **readwrite on the whole bucket** and is **not prefix-scoped**, whereas the current inline policy is tightly scoped to `${AuditLogS3Prefix}/*` and write-only. If prefix-level least-privilege is a hard requirement here (audit-log PII), the team has a legitimate reason to deviate — but that deviation should be an explicit, commented decision, not the default. Recommend confirming with the platform team whether the access manager can scope to a prefix; if not, keep the inline policy but add a comment explaining why the custom resource's S3 Target was rejected. As written, it reads like an oversight.

**References**: `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md:147,181` (Targets grant readwrite S3 access).

---

## Medium

### Issue 2: `delete_logs` loads all keys into memory before batching — unbounded for large audit logs
**Severity**: Medium
**Category**: Performance
**File**: `audit_logging.py:165-192`

**Explanation**: `delete_logs` paginates the full query into a single `keys: list[...]` before chunking. For a request with a very large audit history this materialises every key in memory before the first delete is issued. More importantly, the query and the deletes are fully serialised: all pages are read, *then* all batches are written. You can stream: accumulate keys until you have 25, flush, repeat. This bounds memory to one batch and overlaps read/delete. Given audit logs per erasure are likely small (tens of entries), this is Medium not High — but the pattern is the kind that bites later when one request accumulates thousands of entries.

**Current Code**:
```python
keys: list[dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef]] = []
for page in pages:
    for item in page.get("Items", []):
        keys.append({ ... })
for i in range(0, len(keys), DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS):
    chunk = keys[i : i + DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS]
    request_items = {self.table_name: [{"DeleteRequest": {"Key": key}} for key in chunk]}
    self._batch_write_with_retry(request_items)
```

**Improved Version** — stream keys into fixed-size batches:
```python
batch: list[dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef]] = []
for page in pages:
    for item in page.get("Items", []):
        batch.append({
            AuditEntry.ID_FIELD: item[AuditEntry.ID_FIELD],
            AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD: item[AuditEntry.TIMESTAMP_FIELD],
        })
        if len(batch) == DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS:
            self._flush_delete_batch(batch)
            batch = []
if batch:
    self._flush_delete_batch(batch)
```
where `_flush_delete_batch` wraps the `request_items` construction + `_batch_write_with_retry` call (this also satisfies §3m extraction — see Issue 4).

---

### Issue 3: Module-level constants defined *below* the function that uses them
**Severity**: Medium
**Category**: Readability / Correctness
**File**: `audit_logging.py:187,198,206-207` (uses) vs `352-355` (definitions)

**Explanation**: `DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS`, `DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS`, `BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS`, `BACKOFF_MAX_SECONDS` are referenced inside `DynamoDBAuditLogger.delete_logs` / `_batch_write_with_retry` (lines 187-208) but are not defined until lines 352-360 — *after* the class. This works at runtime only because the methods aren't called at import time; Python resolves the global lazily on first call. It's legal but fragile and confusing: a reader at line 187 has to scroll past the class to find the value, and any import-time use (e.g. a default-argument expression, or a future module-level invocation) would `NameError`. `_backoff_seconds` (line 358) is similarly defined after `_batch_write_with_retry` which calls it. Move the constants and `_backoff_seconds` above the `DynamoDBAuditLogger` class.

**Current Code** (definitions at 352-360, far below first use at 187):
```python
DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ITEMS: Final[int] = 25  # AWS BatchWriteItem hard limit per call
DYNAMO_BATCH_WRITE_MAX_ATTEMPTS: Final[int] = 5
BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS: Final[float] = 0.1
BACKOFF_MAX_SECONDS: Final[float] = 2.0

def _backoff_seconds(attempt: int) -> float: ...
```

**Improved Version**: relocate these four constants and `_backoff_seconds` to immediately after the imports (around line 27), before `class DynamoDBAuditLogger`.

---

### Issue 4: Delete-batch construction is an extractable concern
**Severity**: Medium
**Category**: Readability (§3m inline-logic extraction)
**File**: `audit_logging.py:187-192`

**Explanation**: The chunk loop owns one distinct concern — "turn N keys into one or more 25-item BatchWriteItem delete requests" — and it would have a clear name. Extracting it (`_flush_delete_batch` / `_delete_keys`) both names the concern and makes the streaming refactor in Issue 2 land in one place. This is the lighter of the §3m findings; it pairs naturally with Issue 2's fix. Severity Medium because the block sits in operational delete code where clarity matters during an incident.

---

### Issue 5: Archival is effectively dead code with no end-to-end test through a real `done` transition
**Severity**: Medium
**Category**: Correctness / Test quality
**File**: `request_processor.py:218-248`; `tests/test_request_processor.py` (archiver tests)

**Explanation**: The docstring (lines 230-233) is honest: no production handler reaches `"done"`. Every archiver test (`test_archiver_called_when_request_reaches_done`, etc.) monkeypatches `process_phase1` to fake the `"done"` transition. That validates the wiring in `_archive_if_done` but **not** the actual contract the precondition relies on: "`process_request` was the call that transitioned to `done`". When phase-3 is eventually completed to transition to `"done"`, there's no test guaranteeing `_archive_if_done` fires off the *real* path — and the `process_phase3` handler at line 497 already does `self._transition_request(request, "done")`. So the precondition is in fact already satisfiable on this branch via `process_phase3`, but only if a request is in `phase-3` and the customer is absent. Recommend one integration-style test that drives a `phase-3` request through the real `process_phase3` → `done` → archive path (using `InMemoryAuditLogger` + a mock archiver), rather than faking phase-1. This closes the gap between the test path and the documented precondition. Severity Medium because the redundancy is masking a missing real-path edge-case test (§3i).

---

## Polish (Low)

- **`audit_logging.py:194-195`** — `_batch_write_with_retry`'s parameter uses a string annotation `"dict[str, list[WriteRequestTypeDef]]"` while `delete_logs:189` uses the bare form `dict[str, list[WriteRequestTypeDef]]`. Both work (the type is `TYPE_CHECKING`-only), but pick one form for consistency within the file. Prefer the bare form since `WriteRequestTypeDef` is already imported under `TYPE_CHECKING`.
- **`audit_logging.py:200`** — `response.get("UnprocessedItems") or {}` doubly guards (missing key → `None` → `{}`, and empty dict → `{}`). Correct and arguably defensive, but `response.get("UnprocessedItems", {})` is the idiomatic single form unless the API can return an explicit `None` (it doesn't). Minor.
- **`audit_logging.py:25-26 / 352`** — `_s3_key_for` (line 239) and the constants block (line 352) leave the module's ordering mixed (helpers, classes, then constants, then more factory functions). Consider grouping constants at top (ties into Issue 3).
- **`creator_lambda.py:170`** — the broad `except Exception` after the `DuplicateRequestError` catch is correct (returns 500), and `test_lambda_handler_returns_500_on_other_client_error` covers it. Good. No change needed; noted as a positive.
- **`request_repository.py:25-31`** — `DuplicateRequestError` shadows nothing but is defined in `request_repository` and re-exported via `creator_lambda` import; the duplicate class docstring in the diff vs the file is identical — fine. The `id` shadowing the builtin is consistent with the rest of the file's style.

---

## Summary

**Overall Assessment**: Solid, well-tested feature work. The S3-first-then-delete ordering is deliberately correct and the failure-mode reasoning in the `AuditArchiver` docstring is excellent. The dominant theme in the findings is one real convention violation (inline IAM on a managed role) plus a cluster of code-organisation/clarity nits in `audit_logging.py`.

**Priority Action List**:
1. **Issue 1 (High)** — replace `ProcessS3WritePolicy` with an `S3` Target on `ProcessServerlessAccessManager`, OR add an explicit comment justifying the prefix-scoped deviation. Confirm prefix-scoping capability with the platform team.
2. **Issue 3 (Medium)** — move the four `DYNAMO_*`/`BACKOFF_*` constants and `_backoff_seconds` above `DynamoDBAuditLogger` to remove the define-after-use fragility.
3. **Issue 2 + 4 (Medium)** — stream `delete_logs` keys into fixed batches via an extracted `_flush_delete_batch` helper.
4. **Issue 5 (Medium)** — add one test driving the real `process_phase3 → done → archive` path.

**Positive Observations**:
- The `AuditArchiver` ordering contract (S3 write before DynamoDB delete, with retry-safety and "never returns empty" reasoning) is genuinely well thought through and documented at `audit_logging.py:329-336`. The `test_audit_log_preserved_in_dynamodb_when_s3_write_fails` test (using `moto` + a failing S3 mock) verifies exactly the invariant the docstring claims — that is the right test to have written.
- `DuplicateRequestError` decoupling (`request_repository.py:25-31`, caught in `creator_lambda.py:167-169`) cleanly removes `ClientError`-code sniffing from the handler, and both repo implementations raise it consistently. The 409-vs-500 distinction is tested on both the `ConditionalCheckFailedException` and the `ProvisionedThroughputExceededException` paths.
- `_backoff_seconds` correctly applies the §3m guidance from a prior review — `2.0 ** attempt` with named `Final` constants and a docstring stating the sequence, rather than a bitshift. Good carry-forward.

**Scorecard**:

| Dimension | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Formatting compliance | Pass | Python; `basedpyright` + test suite clean |
| Type precision | Pass | Test-only `cast`/`object` boundary casts are appropriate for boto3 stubs; no production `Any` leakage |
| Test redundancy | Minor issues | Archiver tests all fake phase-1; real `done`-path test missing (Issue 5) |
| README sync | In sync | `POST /erasure-request`, audit lifecycle, and process env vars all newly documented and match code |
| Paradigm adherence | Issues found | Inline IAM vs serverless-access-manager S3 Target (Issue 1) |
| Extensibility risk | Low | New variants (sources/sinks) are cleanly isolated behind `S3AuditLogWriter`/`AuditArchiver` |
| Inline-logic clarity | Issues found | 1 extractable block (delete batching, Issue 4); constants-after-use ordering (Issue 3) |

One memory worth recording: the team repeatedly attaches inline `AWS::IAM::Policy` resources to serverless-access-manager-managed roles instead of using Source/Target entries. I'll note that for future SAM reviews in this org.

---
name: inline-iam-vs-sam-targets
description: This org's SAM templates repeatedly attach inline AWS::IAM::Policy to serverless-access-manager-managed roles instead of using S3/DynamoDB/etc Targets
metadata:
  type: project
---

In dns-de-* / data-rte-* SAM templates, the team has a recurring habit of attaching standalone `AWS::IAM::Policy` resources (with `Roles: [!GetAtt XxxServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleName]`) to grant S3/Secrets/SSM access, instead of declaring those as `Sources`/`Targets` on the `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` resource. Seen in dns-de-rte-orchestrator template.yaml: `ProcessS3WritePolicy` (new in DATA-4715), `ProcessSecretsSSMPermsPolicy`, `ReporterS3ReadPolicy`.

**Why:** The access-manager custom resource owns and reconciles the execution role; external policy attachments conflict with its reconciliation loop (playbook §"No inline IAM"). BUT the team sometimes does it deliberately because the S3 Target grants readwrite on the whole bucket and is NOT prefix-scoped — and they want prefix-level least-privilege for PII audit logs.

**How to apply:** Flag inline IAM on a managed role as High per the playbook, but always note the prefix-scoping tradeoff: the access-manager S3 Target (`Bucket` only) cannot scope to a key prefix. If the deviation is intentional for least-privilege, the fix is a one-line justifying comment, not removal. Ask whether prefix-scoping is a hard requirement before insisting on the Target. See [[serverless-access-manager review playbook]].

content.text:
Review complete. Summary of what I found:

**One High finding** — `template.yaml:204-215`: the new `ProcessS3WritePolicy` is an inline `AWS::IAM::Policy` attached to the serverless-access-manager-managed role, which conflicts with the custom resource's reconciliation loop. The canonical fix is an `S3` Target on `ProcessServerlessAccessManager` — but I flagged the genuine tradeoff that the access-manager S3 Target grants whole-bucket readwrite and can't prefix-scope, so if the prefix-scoped PII least-privilege is intentional the fix is a justifying comment, not removal.

**Four Medium findings** in `audit_logging.py` and the processor tests — unbounded key materialisation in `delete_logs`, constants defined after first use, an extractable delete-batch helper (§3m), and archiver tests that all fake the phase-1 transition rather than exercising the real `process_phase3 → done → archive` path.

**Verification**: full suite passes (208 tests), `basedpyright` clean, README is in sync with the new endpoint/lifecycle/env-var changes.

The feature itself is well-built — the S3-first-then-delete ordering, its failure-mode docstring, and the `DuplicateRequestError` decoupling are all solid, and the `_backoff_seconds` helper correctly applies prior §3m guidance.

I recorded one cross-project memory: this org repeatedly attaches inline IAM policies to access-manager-managed roles, with the prefix-scoping tradeoff to weigh before flagging.
